content.text: Handling Rules by Data Classification Tier

Once data has been assigned a sensitivity label, the label determines a specific set of handling requirements that govern how the data may be processed, stored, transmitted, and reviewed. These handling rules are not suggestions — they are the operational expression of your data governance policy, and they must be enforced consistently across every Claude deployment that touches classified data.

attrs.alt: Handling rules matrix — five handling dimensions across four classification tiers from PUBLIC to RESTRICTED

content.text: PUBLIC data is information that is already in the public domain, or that your organization actively intends to publish. It carries the fewest handling constraints but still benefits from a documented policy to prevent scope creep — data that was once public may be reclassified when combined with other information.
Send to any Claude model tier (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus) without additional controls
No requirement for zero-data-retention API mode
Store Claude outputs without encryption requirement (standard storage security applies)
No mandatory human review before downstream use
No mandatory audit logging (optional for analytics purposes)
May be used in fine-tuning datasets without additional data governance review

# Public data may be freely used in Claude prompts
# but must be re-classified after assembly if combined with non-public content

from typing import NamedTuple

class LabeledContent(NamedTuple):
    text: str
    label: str  # "PUBLIC", "INTERNAL", "CONFIDENTIAL", "RESTRICTED"

def check_public_contamination(chunks: list[LabeledContent]) -> str:
    """
    Even if all source content is PUBLIC, check whether the assembled
    prompt or its output could generate non-public information.
    Returns effective label for the assembled content.
    """
    labels = [c.label for c in chunks]
    order = ["PUBLIC", "INTERNAL", "CONFIDENTIAL", "RESTRICTED"]
    return max(labels, key=lambda l: order.index(l))

INTERNAL data is the most common classification in enterprise Claude deployments. Most team communications, strategy documents, and non-customer business data falls into this tier. The key constraint at INTERNAL is that prompt content must not be logged by the vendor — this requires the Enterprise API tier.

Enterprise API only. The standard API (used without an enterprise agreement) may log prompts for safety review — this is not acceptable for INTERNAL data. Confirm zero-prompt-logging in your contract.
Your own application must not log the full prompt text in plain text application logs. Log request metadata (timestamp, model, token counts, use case ID) but not content.

API keys for INTERNAL workloads must be stored in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault) — never in environment variables in shared CI/CD systems or in version control.

Claude outputs generated from INTERNAL inputs are themselves INTERNAL. Apply the output label before storing or distributing. Do not share INTERNAL-labeled outputs with external parties without an explicit downgrade decision.

# Deployment configuration checklist for INTERNAL data workloads
api_configuration:
  tier: enterprise          # Not standard/free tier
  prompt_logging: disabled  # Confirmed in contract addendum
  api_key_storage: vault    # Not env vars or code

application_controls:
  log_content: false        # Log metadata only
  log_metadata:
    - timestamp
    - model_id
    - input_token_count
    - output_token_count
    - use_case_id
    - request_id

output_handling:
  label_outputs: true       # Tag every output with INTERNAL label
  encrypt_at_rest: false    # Not required at INTERNAL (standard security applies)
  external_sharing: requires_downgrade_decision

audit:
  enabled: true
  retain_days: 365  # 1-year retention for INTERNAL audit logs

CONFIDENTIAL data requires materially stronger controls than INTERNAL. The defining characteristic of CONFIDENTIAL is that its disclosure would cause significant business harm — unreleased financial results, M&A targets, trade secrets, detailed customer analytics. At this tier, zero-data-retention (ZDR) API mode is required, and outputs must be reviewed by a human before external distribution.

The Anthropic Enterprise API must be called with the zero-data-retention flag set in the contract. In ZDR mode, Anthropic does not log prompt content or responses for safety review or model training. Verify this is active in your contract addendum, not just assumed.

CONFIDENTIAL workloads should use an API key separate from INTERNAL workloads. This creates an audit boundary: if a CONFIDENTIAL key is compromised, the blast radius is limited to CONFIDENTIAL workloads.

Any output generated from CONFIDENTIAL inputs must be reviewed by an authorized person before being distributed externally. This review gate must be enforced in the application workflow — policy-only enforcement is insufficient.
Stored outputs from CONFIDENTIAL workloads must be encrypted at rest (AES-256 or equivalent). Apply access controls limiting who can read stored outputs.
Legal review of new use cases:
Before adding a new CONFIDENTIAL use case to the model catalog, require a legal review. This is not required for INTERNAL use cases.

import anthropic

# Verify that zero-data-retention mode is active for your organization
# by checking the API response headers (Anthropic sets a header when ZDR is active)

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def verify_zdr_active() -> bool:
    """
    Send a test request and check whether the response indicates ZDR mode.
    In enterprise ZDR configurations, Anthropic includes response metadata
    confirming the data handling mode.
    """
    # The actual ZDR verification mechanism depends on your Anthropic contract.
    # Contact your account team to confirm how ZDR mode is signaled in API responses.
    # This is a placeholder pattern — do not rely on it without account team confirmation.
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "ping"}],
    )
    # In practice: check resp.model, response headers, or use the Anthropic admin API
    # to verify the key's data handling policy
    return True  # Replace with actual verification

assert verify_zdr_active(), "ZDR mode not confirmed — do not process CONFIDENTIAL data until verified"

RESTRICTED data is regulated personal information (PII, PHI, payment card data) or the organization's highest-sensitivity business data. Every additional safeguard available must be applied at this tier. The regulatory dimension — GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, CCPA — creates specific legal obligations that go beyond technical controls.
Signed Data Processing Agreement covering all data processing activities
Business Associate Agreement required if any Protected Health Information is processed
Separate key from CONFIDENTIAL and INTERNAL workloads; principle of least privilege
Zero-data-retention confirmed in contract for this key
Remove or redact PII from prompts where the task allows (e.g., summarization)
Every output reviewed before any use — no automated downstream processing
AES-256 at rest; TLS 1.2+ in transit; separate encryption keys per data subject class
Role-based access; only authorized roles may read stored RESTRICTED outputs
Full audit log retained per applicable regulation (7 years for HIPAA, up to 6 years for GDPR)
Mechanism to delete stored outputs when data subject requests deletion

import anthropic, hashlib, datetime

class RestrictedDataHandler:
    """
    Enforces RESTRICTED handling rules for Claude API calls.
    All methods log to the audit system and enforce human review gates.
    """

    def __init__(self, audit_logger, human_review_queue):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        self.audit = audit_logger
        self.review_queue = human_review_queue

    def process(
        self,
        prompt: str,
        model: str,
        use_case_id: str,
        data_subjects: list[str],  # IDs for right-to-erasure tracking
        requester_id: str,
    ) -> str:
        """
        Process a RESTRICTED prompt and route output through human review.
        Returns a review ticket ID, not the output directly.
        """
        prompt_hash = hashlib.sha256(prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

        resp = self.client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        output_text = resp.content[0].text

        # Log audit entry — never log prompt or output content
        self.audit.write({
            "ts": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
            "use_case": use_case_id,
            "model": model,
            "requester": requester_id,
            "prompt_hash": prompt_hash,
            "output_hash": hashlib.sha256(output_text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16],
            "data_subjects": data_subjects,  # for erasure tracking
            "label": "RESTRICTED",
        })

        # Route to human review queue — do not return output directly
        ticket_id = self.review_queue.submit(
            output=output_text,
            context={"use_case": use_case_id, "requester": requester_id},
        )
        return ticket_id  # caller polls review queue, not this method
Cross-Tier Rules: Downgrading and Upgrading Labels

Data governance programs need a formal process for changing a label — both upgrading (when content turns out to be more sensitive than originally classified) and downgrading (when RESTRICTED content is anonymized sufficiently to move it to a lower tier).
Submit a downgrade request documenting: original label, proposed label, method of de-sensitization (redaction, anonymization, aggregation), and the use case requiring the downgrade.
The data steward for the relevant data domain reviews the request and confirms the de-sensitization is technically sufficient.
Legal reviews if the data is regulated (PII/PHI downgrade requires confirming re-identification risk is below threshold).
Upon approval, the downgraded copy is created and labeled; the original is not relabeled.
The downgrade decision is recorded in the governance register with a reference to the use case.
Output scan detects PII or PHI in a response generated from lower-classified inputs (Claude synthesized sensitive content)
New information reveals that a previously INTERNAL data source contains regulated personal data
A data subject requests access or deletion, revealing the subject is identifiable from previously INTERNAL data
Regulatory guidance changes the classification of a data type (e.g., IP address newly classified as PII in a jurisdiction)

When a handling rule violation is detected — the wrong model was used, an output was distributed without human review, a RESTRICTED prompt was logged in plain text — the incident response must be calibrated to the tier of the data involved.
Correct the process; no regulatory obligation
Identify affected data, correct process, update audit log
Identify affected data, assess business harm, remediate, report to execs
DPO within 24h; regulatory notification per GDPR/HIPAA timelines
Full breach response per incident playbook; regulatory reporting as required

Handling rules translate sensitivity labels into operational requirements. PUBLIC data flows freely through any Claude model configuration; INTERNAL requires the Enterprise API with no prompt logging; CONFIDENTIAL requires zero-data-retention mode, a dedicated API key, encrypted output storage, and human review before external distribution; RESTRICTED adds DPA and BAA requirements, output human review for all use cases, regulated retention periods, and right-to-erasure support. Enforce these rules through technical controls in the API gateway and application layer — policy-only enforcement fails at scale. Build label downgrade and upgrade processes to handle the cases where classification changes after initial assignment.
